title: Study Abroad in Bremen, Germany
description: While working toward my Bachelor's degree in Information Systems at UMSL, I applied for a study abroad program in Germany. My time in Germany was transformative, and it solidified my desire to move to Europe.

During this time, I also visited the Netherlands for the first time, which sparked my interest in relocating there. I was even able to fly two of my younger sisters to spend a few days with me in the Netherlands, and they loved it too.
The program ended and I returned to the U.S. in July 2024, but I knew I wanted to return to Europe permanently. I made the decision to start planning my move to the Netherlands.

links.text: My short interview about studying abroad

title: Downsizing and preparing for the move
description: Upon returning from Germany and with a clear vision of my future in the Netherlands, I began the process of downsizing my belongings. I immediately sold my car, which was my way of confirming for myself that I was serious about this move.

It was the first step in a series of logistical preparations that included selling or donating furniture, clothes, and other possessions that no longer fit my future. I also transferred to the smallest apartment in my current building, not only slashing $1000 off my monthly rent, but forcing me to live with less "stuff".
I set the goal to move with one single bag.

title: Researching visas and work options
description: Admittedly, I knew very little about visas at this point. I started researching the various visa options available for moving to the Netherlands. It took about a week of research before I found the Dutch American Friendship Treaty (DAFT) visa.

This visa was appealing because it allowed me to start a business in the Netherlands and live there for up to 2 years, with the possibility of extension. The business can be a simple sole proprietorship, which suited my needs as a software developer. I easily met the requirements for the DAFT visa, and the work requirements (entrepreneurship) aligned perfectly with my background.

I was already freelancing/contracting and had clients, which would help me meet the income requirements for the visa. At this point, it's just another one of my several moves to a new city (but this time across the Atlantic Ocean).

links.text: Becoming a ZZP'er in the Netherlands

title: Nederlands leren (learning Dutch)
description: I don't care how much of the Dutch population speaks English. Learning the local language is a sign of respect and a way to integrate into society.

I started learning Dutch in July 2024, using the app Duolingo. It was the easiest and most obvious way to get started. I supplemented my learning with YouTube videos, books, podcasts, ChatGPT and Dutch courses. I also started listening to Dutch music and watching Dutch TV shows with English subtitles to immerse myself in the language.

By the end of 2024, I was a strong A2 level in Dutch with B1 so close I could taste it. Ik kan met zekerheid zeggen dat ik een beetje Nederlands spreek. Het is een moeilijke taal maar ik studeer en oefen elke dag. Geen Google Translate.

links.text: My Dutch R&B/Vibes playlist on YouTube

title: City shopping in the Netherlands
description: Up to this point, I had been to several cities in both Germany and the Netherlands (NL). However, in NL, I had only spent a decent amount of time in Amsterdam. I wasn't convinced that was my only option.

So, I flew back to NL for the sole purpose of "city shopping." Alongside another one of my sisters I spent several days exploring Rotterdam — a city I knew I would enjoy after plenty of research. I also visited and explored Utrecht.

There was no doubt in my mind that Rotterdam was my speed. Plus, considering how small the Netherlands is, I could easily travel anywhere in the country within a few hours by train. I decided Rotterdam was the perfect fit for me. My sister agreed.

title: Savings target in euros
description: From the outside looking in, I still live in the U.S. In my mind, the move has already begun. That means I would need to make decisions as though I was in the middle of moving, not as if the move was coming in the future.

I set a savings target of €14,500, which is €4,500 for the DAFT visa business account reserve plus an additional €10,000 for securing an apartment and paying any fees. I actually set this savings target in October during my visit to Rotterdam.

Because I had already sold my car and downsized my living expenses significantly, I was able to save aggressively. I opened a Wise account to hold my Euros. I am already the type to track my finances meticulously using Excel. So, every time money came in, I already knew exactly how much of it to transfer, leaving the rest for my U.S. expenses.

links.text: DAFT visa requirements (see the business account reserve)

title: Purchased 1-Way flight for September 2025
description: On May 30, 2025, I booked my flight to the Netherlands for September 22, 2025 — the day my U.S. apartment lease ends. I'll turn in my keys and head straight to the airport. 🎉

After arriving in the Netherlands on the 23rd, I'll stay in a hotel for the remainder of September. On October 1st, I'll check into a short-term rental in Rotterdam. These types of rentals allow address registration (inschrijven, meaning you can inform the local municipality that this is your address), which is necessary for the DAFT visa application.

With a registered address, I can secure a BSN (Dutch social security number), open a Dutch business bank account, and register my business as a sole proprietorship (ZZP'er) with the Dutch Chamber of Commerce (Kamer van Koophandel).

With a registered address, BSN, and €4,500 deposited into the business account, I can officially apply for the DAFT visa. Approval is nearly guaranteed. Once approved, I live in the Netherlands. That's it. Done.

title: Secured short-term accommodation in Rotterdam
description: With less than 90 days until my move, I performed another search for short-term housing — this time with intent to book.

I was introduced to an organization called The Social Hub, a collection of extended-stay hotels across Europe. They have a location in Rotterdam that offers short-term rentals with the ability to register your address (inschrijven). It's designed specifically for those needing temporary housing that can be treated like a permanent home.
I booked an extended-stay studio through the end of 2025, which will allow me to register my address with the municipality and apply for the DAFT visa.
If all goes well, my DAFT visa will be approved during my stay at The Social Hub, and I will be searching for a permanent apartment in Rotterdam during that period.

title: Boots on the ground in Rotterdam
description: While the plan isn't fully executed until I have an approved DAFT visa, I can't help but celebrate this major milestone. 🎉 As of September 23, 2025, me and all of my worldly possessions are in Rotterdam, Netherlands — with no return flight to the U.S. booked or planned.

I am in a temporary hotel for the first 8 days and will be moving to The Social Hub on October 1st. From here, I will be applying for my BSN (Dutch social security number), opening a Dutch business bank account, registering my business with the Kamer van Koophandel (Dutch Chamber of Commerce), and applying for my DAFT visa.

The extended stay hotel allows me to register a personal address, which puts me on the path to meet all the requirements for the DAFT visa. The next few months will be busy, but I am excited to finally be here and start this new chapter of my life.

title: BSN (BurgerServiceNummer) acquired
description: As part of the process for becoming a resident of the Netherlands, you must register your presence in one of their municipalities and acquire a BSN — similar to a U.S. social security number. The Dutch government uses it to identify and interact with you.

It is the key to multiple tasks: opening a Dutch bank account, purchasing a cell phone subscription, registering your business, and applying for a visa. I acquired my BSN near the end of November 2025, two months after arriving in Rotterdam.

Within 48 hours of receipt, I was able to open a Dutch bank account and log into all government websites. What an insanely efficient process if you follow the steps properly. From here, I will continue moving through the DAFT visa process, which is well underway.

title: Residence permit acquired - mission complete 🥳
description: On 23 December 2025 — 91 days after arriving in the Netherlands — I was granted permission to stay until at least October 2027 under the DAFT visa. I received my physical residence permit on 16 January 2026.
With a residence permit in hand, I have proof that I am allowed to be here. That opens the door to things like renting an apartment, having a gym membership, and obviously much more.

Mission complete. Now it's time to establish myself as an independent web developer in the Netherlands. My successes and failures over the next 20 months or so will have a direct impact on my ability to extend my visa when the time comes. Laten we gaan!

title: I got an apartment 🏠
description: It finally happened. After months of applications, very few viewings, and a lot of patience, I got an offer accepted on an apartment in Rotterdam.

Leaving The Social Hub after several months was bittersweet — it was genuinely a great place to land during an uncertain time. But there's no substitute for your own space. Unpacking what fit into one bag and spreading it across an apartment on a lease that's yours is a different feeling entirely.
This feels like the real beginning. Not the visa, not the BSN, not even the residence permit — the apartment. Having a place that is genuinely mine, in the city I chose, is what makes it all land.

title: Apartment hunting in the Netherlands is not for the weak 😰
description: The Netherlands has a serious housing shortage, especially in popular cities like Rotterdam and Amsterdam. This isn't new information, but it's something I didn't fully grasp until I started apartment hunting in early 2026. Wow.

The process is brutal. Apartments are scarce, expensive, and highly competitive. To date, I have applied to over three dozen listings. This has led to three viewings and zero offers accepted. I knew it would be difficult, but nothing can prepare you for the reality of it until you're in the thick of it. It's a full-time job just applying to apartments.

The good news is that I still have a roof over my head at The Social Hub. I am extremely comfortable, but the clock is ticking. My target apartment would cost roughly €1,200 less than my current temporary housing, so the financial incentive is there. I just need to get lucky and find the right fit. In the meantime, I will continue applying and hope for the best.
